author: @owengrossman1414
text: I spent a bit of time addressing this problem in Fusion 360.  I was hoping that you would at least allude to the issue of gear tooth geometry.  I came to the conclusion that it’s impossible to design a tooth shape with precision meshing because the curvature of the spiral is not constant.  In other words spiral gearing is not really a viable engineering solution.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Personally I think that's a bit beyond the scope of a 'how to do it' tinkercad tutorial - so your expectations might have been a bit high lol. I think your conclusion is also a bit odd as this kind of system has many practical uses - a lathe chuck being what i think of as good example of its use - but maybe we are thinking about different things here

author: @benbeazley3614
text: I was just doing a spiral for the first time this morning in FreeCAD. It is much easier but each to their own tools. 😊 There are definitely learning curves to each!

author: @grendel1960a
text: continue the spiral around a few more times and you could easily construct a scroll chuck for a lathe, the limit to the number of jaws would be hom many you could fit around your chuck, and since a chuck uses a square tooth pattern you dont have limitations on tooth shape. (of course 3d printed limits you to the strength of your filamment, but you could send it to one of the fabricators to be done in a sintered metal.) though it would create a soft jaw chuck for small delicate objects.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i like that idea - cheers mate

author: @lowrads
text: The universe seems to love spirals and ellipses.  A conic spiral would be pretty tricky to make on a conventional lathe.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it does indeed mate

author: @ianbottom7396
text:
Very nice Robert.
Perhaps you could also use the concept to create custom partial output shaft rotation with a profile that starts and finishes at the same point, perhaps there’s an easier solution but in theory it would work 😂

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: That would be cool

author: @SAILBOATJACARANDA
text: Completely random, but I was wondering if I could have your thoughts. I'm intending to use a phosphate primer then epoxy paint for my steel boat. I see a lot (most) use a rust converter prior to primer. Do you think the rust converter is a needed step? And if so, rust converter to my understanding is basically just tannic acid, so could I just buy powder and mix it with water instead?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I thought most effective rust converter also contained phosphoric acid mate - buit i can't really advise on that as i don't know that much about boats

replies.author: @grendel1960a
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering apparently molasses is also a good rust converter, never tried it myself, the process of converting the rust prior to painting is quite a common one on steel hulled boats, but also an enclosed section within the steelwork allows for the rust to start forming and then sealing the section, once the iron oxide has formed and used up the oxygen in the space, then no more rust can form unless additional oxygen is introduced, so this principle is used a lot on steel boats too. for an in depth look, I am sure project Brupeg has covered the topic somewhere in the past (both the rust conversion and the sealed pockets)

replies.author: @SAILBOATJACARANDA
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering cheers

replies.author: @SAILBOATJACARANDA
replies.text: ​@grendel1960a yeah fan of Brupeg and Dangar Marine whos their neighbour got loads of great videos on the topic. Sv seeker haad a massive metal paint library en all. Mweh Ill blow off the wallet cobwebs and buy some rust converter.

text:
Very interesting.   Thanks for the video.   I have been pondering how to make a jig which will allow me to create spiral flutes on my wood lathe.    This may be a potential solution.    The spiral mounted to the chuck then using the gear to move a carriage with e.g., router down the work.   Hmmmm..
Dave.

author: @jamesross1003
text: This brings back memories of a class I loved in college.  It was CIS Algorithms.  It was a real eye opener and delved really deeply into logic behind a great many things and how to make unique iterations of one thing to accomplish a job or solve a problem(a good example of this was a project we were assigned to approximate PI by throwing random darts at a dart board via a computer program).  I would have thought you could use a screw shape and then compress it to make a spiral, but I like your approach using the underlying mathematics as it makes the concept to make any new shape in the program.  Great idea Rob!  Thanks for the video!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am glad you liked it mate - cheers

author: @bartronicsecurity
text: Is there a reason you made the diameter of the spiral gear so big? As I see it a smaller diameter will still have the same gear ratio as a big one.
